front: Poem: the refrain 'Write down! / I am an Arab' is repeated throughout
Poem: ends with the warning 'Beware / Of my hunger / And my anger'
Poem: the speaker mentions having eight children, with a ninth on the way, and asks 'Will you be angry?'
Poem: the number 'fifty thousand' is printed on the title object
Poem: the speaker has 'a name without a title'
Poem: the speaker claims 'I do not hate people / Nor do I encroach / But if I become hungry / The usurper's flesh will be my food'
Poem: Israel's defense minister Avigdor Lieberman controversially likened this poem to Mein Kampf in 2016
Poem: 'Nothing is left for us and my grandchildren / Except these rocks'
Prose poem: set on Hiroshima Day during the 1982 siege of Beirut
Prose poem: the narrator yearns for the 'aroma of coffee' and declares 'Coffee is geography'
Prose poem: uses imagery of doves and coffee to discuss fear; ends 'I don't see a shore. / I don't see a dove.'
Prose poem: opens 'Out of one dream, another dream is born'
Prose poem: links the violence of the Lebanese Civil War to the bombing of Hiroshima on August 6, 1945
Poem: set 'within the ancient walls' of Jerusalem
Poem: the speaker walks 'from one epoch to another without a memory to guide me'
Poem: a 'woman soldier' shouts 'Didn't I kill you?' and the speaker replies 'You killed me... and I forgot, like you, to die'
Poem: the speaker claims 'I don't walk, I fly, I become another, / transfigured' and describes hands as 'two doves / on the cross hovering and carrying the earth'
Poem: prophets return from heaven 'less discouraged and melancholy, because love and peace are holy and are coming to town'
Collection: contains the refrain 'One day I shall become what I want' and the lines 'This is your name-- / a woman said, / and vanished through the winding of a corridor'
Poem: 'A Soldier Dreams of White Lilies' compares love to a glass of wine
back: "A Soldier Dreams of White Lilies" (Mahmoud Darwish)

front: Poem: in 'The Horse Fell Off the Poem,' the title animal became 'bloodied with my poem' due to the inadequacy of language
back: "The Horse Fell Off the Poem" (Mahmoud Darwish)

front: Poem: 'you and I are the absent ones' appears in a poem where a horse 'fell off the poem'
back: "The Horse Fell Off the Poem" (Mahmoud Darwish)

front: Collection: titled for a body of water that 'Dies of Thirst'
back: A River Dies of Thirst (Mahmoud Darwish)

front: Poem: in the final line of 'The Earth is Closing on Us,' the speaker's blood gives birth to olive trees
back: "The Earth is Closing on Us" (Mahmoud Darwish)

front: Poem: 'The Second Olive Tree' is about a martyred grandmother and her sons; olive trees symbolize the Palestinian concept of resilience called sumud
Collection: Victims of a Map is a collaborative volume with Samih al-Qasim and Adonis, containing 'We Fear for a Dream'
Collection: The Butterfly's Burden contains a poem titled for the 'Damascene Collar of the Dove'
Poem: in 'Poem for the Land,' carnations grow where 'stones take flight' and 'dewdrops become weapons'
Poem: 'Antithesis' describes meeting Edward Said in New York 'at the edge of an electric chasm sky high'
Poem: the speaker 'learnt all the words and broke them up / To make a single word: Homeland'
Poem: this author wrote about his Jewish lover Rita; one poem begins 'Between Rita and my eyes there is a rifle'
Poem: 'Writing is a puppy biting nothingness / Writing wounds without a trace of blood'
Poem: the speakers 'cultivate hope' while standing 'Close to the gardens of broken shadow'
Author: admired Yehuda Amichai but described him as 'a challenge to me, because we write about the same place'
Author: described competing with Yehuda Amichai to be 'the owner of the language of this land'
Author: Naomi Shihab Nye called this poet the 'Essential Breath' of his ethnic group
Author: Edward Said wrote that 'the personal and the public are always in an uneasy relationship' in this poet's work
Author: wrote the Palestinian Declaration of Independence
Author: Leaves of Olives is an early collection by this poet
